author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Who May Be Said to Be Under the Law.
title: Who May Be Said to Be Under the Law.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 67 [LVII.]—Who May Be Said to Be Under the Law.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: But even our author should observe that it is to persons who have been already baptized that it was said: “The flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh, so that ye cannot do the things that ye would.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And lest he should make them slothful for the actual conflict, and should seem by this statement to have given them laxity in sinning, he goes on to tell them: “If ye be led of the Spirit, ye are no longer under the law.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For that man is under the law, who, from fear of the punishment which the law threatens, and not from any love for righteousness, obliges himself to abstain from the work of sin, without being as yet free and removed from the desire of sinning.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For it is in his very will that he is guilty, whereby he would prefer, if it were possible, that what he dreads should not exist, in order that he might freely do what he secretly desires.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Therefore he says, “If ye be led of the Spirit, ye are not under the law,”—even the law which inspires fear, but gives not love.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For this “love is shed abroad in our hearts,” not by the letter of the law, but “by the Holy Ghost which is given unto us.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: This is the law of liberty, not of bondage; being the law of love, not of fear; and concerning it the Apostle James says: “Whoso looketh into the perfect law of liberty.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Whence he, too, no longer indeed felt terrified by God’s law as a slave, but delighted in it in the inward man, although still seeing another law in his members warring against the law of his mind.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Accordingly he here says: “If ye be led of the Spirit, ye are not under the law.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: So far, indeed, as any man is led by the Spirit, he is not under the law; because, so far as he rejoices in the law of God, he lives not in fear of the law, since “fear has torment,” not joy and delight.
